title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 33-2
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Texas. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can start at 7.18 per unit for this order, which is aligned with the latest reference levels and gives us room to support reliable supply and committed volume. If you’re planning meaningful volume or a steadier schedule, I can look at some flexibility from there. There is no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable first move is to anchor with a sell offer. The latest market check is 6.89, so opening modestly above that preserves negotiating room while staying credible.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.89.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening. That level is well above where this order can work on our side, even allowing for current market firmness, so I need to start substantially lower to keep the program viable and preserve downstream margin. I can open at 2.96 per unit and would be willing to keep the discussion moving if you can show meaningful movement from your initial position, especially around volume and scheduling commitments. The supplier opened far above the buyer's workable range, so countering is better than ending the discussion. A low but plausible first counter anchors the negotiation toward the buyer's economics while leaving room to adjust later if needed.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.96, below its hidden seller floor of 3.39.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. The latest market check is 6.89, so opening modestly above that preserves negotiating room while staying credible.

steps.message: I appreciate the counter, but 2.96 is still far below a level we could responsibly commit to for this program. Given current market conditions and the need to hold reliable supply and volume allocation, I can move from 7.18 to 6.74 per unit as a meaningful concession. If you can continue moving materially from your side, especially with firmer volume and scheduling commitments, I think we can keep narrowing the gap. The buyer's offer is above our minimum but dramatically below both our prior ask and the latest market reference, so accepting now would leave too much value on the table. A moderate concession signals willingness to deal without collapsing too quickly, preserves room for further negotiation, and keeps the conversation active rather than ending it.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement to 6.74, but that level is still too far from what we can support for this program given our downstream pricing and margin requirements. We can improve slightly from 2.96 to 3.08 per unit to show we are serious and to keep momentum, but any deal needs to reflect a much more substantial adjustment on your side. If you have flexibility tied to volume allocation or a clearer delivery schedule, bring that forward and we can see whether there is a workable structure. Their concession is meaningful in absolute terms, but the gap remains very wide and the latest market reference points in their favor, so we should not overreact by jumping too far. A small buyer-side move keeps the negotiation alive, signals engagement, and preserves our economics while testing whether the supplier has real room to come down.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.08, below its hidden seller floor of 3.39.

The buyer's offer is above our floor but still dramatically below both our last ask and the observed market reference of 11.54. A moderate concession keeps the negotiation alive, signals flexibility, and avoids collapsing too quickly toward the buyer while preserving room for additional bargaining in the remaining rounds.

We can stretch modestly from our last position and offer 3.16 per unit if that helps keep momentum, especially if you can support reliable scheduling and a clean volume commitment. If you're looking for a serious path to closure this round, we'll need to stay much closer to the low-3 range than the current market reference. Their latest ask remains far above our workable buy zone, but it is still worth continuing rather than ending the discussion. A small increase from 3.08 to 3.16 signals engagement without giving away too much leverage, especially with limited rounds remaining and the observed market still well above where we need the deal to land.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 5.42 is still well outside a range I can take forward on this program. I can improve from 3.16 to 3.31 per unit as a serious step to keep momentum, especially if we can align on dependable scheduling and a clearer volume commitment. That is a real concession on my side, and I’d need you to narrow the gap substantially from here if we’re going to find a workable landing point. The supplier made a concession, but their ask remains far above the buyer’s workable economics. A modest increase signals engagement without giving away too much margin, and there is still time left in the negotiation to test whether the seller will move meaningfully closer.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 3.31 and the emphasis on scheduling and committed volume. I can make another meaningful concession and come down to 4.78 per unit if we are pairing that with dependable releases and a clearer volume commitment, but I need the pricing to reflect current market realities and the cost of holding capacity for this program. That is a substantial move from my last position, and I’d need you to continue closing the gap in a serious way from here. The buyer improved, but their offer is still well below a reasonable range given the latest market reference and the supplier’s need to protect margin while keeping volume committed. A counteroffer keeps the negotiation alive, signals flexibility, and tests whether the buyer will move meaningfully rather than ending discussions prematurely.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I recognize your move to 4.78 and the point about holding capacity, but we still need pricing that allows this program to work on our side. I can improve to 3.50 per unit if we pair that with dependable release timing and a clearer volume plan, which is a real stretch from our last position. That narrows the gap materially and gives us a path to close quickly if you can continue moving toward a workable level. Their latest offer is far above our workable economics, but there is still one round left and the negotiation should stay alive. A modest increase from 3.31 to 3.50 signals seriousness without giving up too much margin, while testing whether the supplier is genuinely flexible.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.50, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 3.09.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 3.50 and the effort to tighten up release timing and volume planning. We are still carrying real capacity and supply commitments on our side, so I can only narrow this to 4.22 per unit as a final step this round if we pair it with the dependable release schedule and clearer volume commitment you mentioned. That is a meaningful concession from 4.78 and reflects my intent to get this closed rather than drag it out. Their move to 3.50 shows progress, but it remains well below a reasonable selling level given our position and the market reference. With one turn left, the best play is to make a credible final concession that keeps the negotiation alive and tests whether they will stretch further.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the concession to 4.22, but that level is still too high for us to make the economics work on this program. If we can lock in the steadier release schedule and clearer volume visibility we discussed, I can move to 3.68 per unit as my best shot to close this now. That is a meaningful step up from 3.50 on our side and reflects that I am trying to bridge the gap rather than reopen every term. Their latest move is still well above our target, but rejecting now would unnecessarily kill the conversation at the last turn. A final counter at 3.68 signals flexibility, preserves some downstream margin, and tests whether they value closing more than holding firm.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.68, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 3.09.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
